ontology.foodex2_description: Doughnuts and similar fried dough products
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Doughnuts and similar fried dough products) is the closest defensible parent category for this Chinese butter-fried dough twist; no exact code exists for Saa Jung.

serving.natural_language_portion: One mini butter-fried Saa Jung twist, approximately 60 g, as typically served at a Chinese pastry shop in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Doughnuts, cake-type, plain (includes unsugared, enriched) — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry exists for Saa Jung or Chinese butter-fried dough twist in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA Foundation Foods, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy entry for cake-type plain doughnuts (FDC ID 172757), which shares the closest compositional profile: deep-fried enriched wheat dough with sugar and fat. Butter content and sugar coating of Saa Jung suggest slightly higher fat and sugar than the USDA reference; values are adjusted upward modestly for fat_g (+2 g/100g) and sugar_g (+3 g/100g) to reflect the butter filling and sugar-coated surface. Sodium is modestly elevated to reflect salted butter. All adjustments are conservative and documented.

gi_source.title: Doughnut, cake-type — Glycemic Index entry; analogous estimate for Chinese butter-fried dough twist
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Saa Jung or Chinese butter-fried dough twist. GI of 76 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cake-type doughnuts (GI ~76, high category), which is the closest analogous deep-fried enriched wheat dough product. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (76 × 29.1) / 100 ≈ 22.1, where carbohydrate per 60 g serving = 48.5 × 0.60 = 29.1 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~320 mg/100 g) from salted butter filling
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to one mini piece and avoid pairing with other high-sodium foods, as the salted butter filling contributes approximately 192 mg sodium per 60 g serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (~76) and significant sugar and refined carbohydrate content (~48.5 g carbohydrate and ~18 g sugar per 100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should treat this as an occasional indulgence only; the high GI and refined flour base produce a rapid postprandial glucose rise, and the estimated glycemic load of ~22 per serving is clinically significant.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated saturated fat (~9.5 g/100 g) and trans fat (~0.2 g/100 g) from deep-frying and butter
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should consume this product sparingly, as the combination of saturated fat from butter and potential trans fat from deep-frying oil may adversely affect LDL cholesterol levels.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mini Butter-Fried Saa Jung Twist (迷你牛油炸沙翁扭) is a Chinese-origin fried pastry snack sold in Singapore, notably at specialty Chinese pastry shops such as RUXU 入续 糖水铺与包. The name 'Saa Jung' (沙翁) refers to a traditional Chinese deep-fried dough ball or twist, here prepared in a mini elongated twist format. The dough is made using a Chinese starter-process (similar to a pre-fermented or tangzhong-style enriched dough) incorporating wheat flour, milk, caster sugar, eggs, and a generous quantity of butter, which gives the interior a soft, pillowy, and slightly moist crumb. The exterior is deep-fried until golden and then coated with a crispy sugar crust, while the filling contains natural salty butter, creating a distinctive sweet-salty flavour contrast. Nutritionally, this is a calorie-dense snack (~395 kcal/100 g) with significant fat (~21 g/100 g, of which ~9.5 g is saturated), moderate protein (~5.2 g/100 g), and high refined carbohydrate (~48.5 g/100 g) including ~18 g sugar. The glycemic index is estimated at ~76 (high), making it a high-glycemic food unsuitable for frequent consumption by individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or cardiovascular disease. The sodium content (~320 mg/100 g) from salted butter is a consideration for hypertensive individuals. This pastry is best enjoyed as an occasional treat and is not suitable for daily consumption within a calorie-controlled or cardiometabolic health diet.

confidence_reason: No direct nutritional entry for Saa Jung or Chinese butter-fried dough twist was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA Foundation Foods, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Values are adapted from the closest USDA SR Legacy analogue (cake-type plain doughnut, FDC ID 172757) with conservative adjustments for butter and sugar coating. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database doughnut entry. Confidence is medium due to the adapted rather than exact source match.
notes: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cake-type plain doughnut (FDC ID 172757) with modest upward adjustments for fat_g (+2 g/100g) and sugar_g (+3 g/100g) to reflect the butter filling and sugar-coated surface characteristic of Saa Jung. Sodium is elevated to ~320 mg/100g to reflect salted butter. Vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entry for this food category. Vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entry for this food category. Selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entry for this food category. Vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 as fried enriched dough products contain negligible vitamin C; confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy reference. Halal status is unconfirmed as the restaurant RUXU 入续 糖水铺与包 does not carry a confirmed halal certification in publicly available records at time of processing. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database doughnut analogue (GI ~76). GL per serving = (76 × 29.1) / 100 ≈ 22.1 for a 60 g serving.
